skill.name: azure-storage
skill.description: Guidance for choosing and using Azure Storage services, including Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake.
skill.summary: Plan, configure, and use Azure Storage services with Azure MCP, Azure CLI, and SDK quick references.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are documentation examples and false positives. The skill provides Azure Storage guidance, Azure CLI examples, SDK package references, and Microsoft Learn links; no prompt injection, hidden execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown command examples misclassified as execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive. The flagged Azure CLI, package manager, and MCP command strings are shown in Markdown tables and fenced documentation examples; the skill contains no executable script wrapper or automatic command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain Markdown documentation with command examples and code spans. No runtime file in the scanned set invokes a shell or passes user input to a command.

low_findings.title: Azure endpoint placeholders misclassified as suspicious network use
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive. The hardcoded URLs are Microsoft Learn links and placeholder Azure Storage account endpoints used in SDK quick starts, not outbound calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to documented Azure service endpoints or Microsoft documentation. They appear inside examples and links, with no code path that sends local data to an unexpected host.

low_findings.title: Static keyword matches are not security issues in context
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive. The dynamic import, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance alerts come from language syntax or ordinary words in guidance, not from code that loads modules dynamically, uses weak hashes, or probes the host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Go import syntax, best-practice text, and SDK reference prose. I found no MD5 or SHA1 usage, host reconnaissance command, or dynamic code execution behavior.

content.value_statement: Azure Storage choices and SDK usage can be hard to compare across services. This skill gives concise guidance for Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake.
actual_capabilities: Compares Azure Storage services by workload type and access pattern.
Lists preferred Azure MCP storage commands for account, container, and blob operations.
Provides Azure CLI fallback commands for storage account, container, blob upload, and blob download tasks.
Summarizes storage account tiers, blob access tiers, and redundancy options.
Links to SDK quick references for Python, TypeScript, Java, Rust, Go, and .NET examples.
Highlights practical SDK authentication patterns using Azure identity libraries.
limitations: It does not create Azure resources without user-approved tool or CLI execution.
It does not replace full Microsoft Learn documentation for advanced service configuration.
It does not validate live Azure permissions, quotas, billing, or tenant policies by itself.
Some SDK guides are condensed and point to deeper service-specific skills for advanced patterns.

use_cases.title: Choose a Storage Service
use_cases.description: Compare Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake for a new workload.

use_cases.title: Operate Storage Accounts
use_cases.description: Use Azure MCP or Azure CLI guidance to list accounts, containers, blobs, and perform basic transfers.

use_cases.title: Build Storage Integrations
use_cases.description: Find SDK package names, authentication patterns, and quick starts for common Azure Storage services.

prompt_templates.title: Pick the Right Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the best Azure Storage service for [workload]. Compare Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake.

prompt_templates.title: List Storage Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Azure MCP or Azure CLI steps to list storage accounts, containers, and blobs for [subscription or account context].

prompt_templates.title: Plan Blob Access Tiers
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend blob access tiers and lifecycle considerations for data that is accessed [frequency] and retained for [retention period].

prompt_templates.title: Design an SDK Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Azure Storage SDK integration in [language] for [service]. Include authentication, upload or read flow, error handling, and security considerations.

output_examples.input: I need storage for static files and backups with low operational overhead.
output_examples.output: A concise recommendation that selects Blob Storage, explains hot, cool, cold, and archive tier tradeoffs, and lists next operational checks.

output_examples.input: I need to inspect blobs in an existing account.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step operations plan that prefers Azure MCP when available and provides Azure CLI fallback commands.

output_examples.input: I need a Python app to write messages to Azure Queue Storage.
output_examples.output: A development outline that names the Python SDK package, recommends Azure identity authentication, and explains poison message handling.

best_practices: Prefer Azure identity authentication and managed identity over long-lived connection strings for production workloads.
Choose storage tiers and redundancy settings from access frequency, durability needs, recovery goals, and cost constraints.
Use SDK streaming, batching, async clients, and idempotent operations for large or high-throughput workloads.
anti_patterns: Do not use one storage service for every workload without checking access patterns and data model needs.
Do not publish long-lived keys, connection strings, or broad SAS tokens in application code or prompts.
Do not use archive or cold tiers for data that needs immediate low-latency access.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure CLI commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Tool or CLI execution still requires the user environment and approval flow.

faq.question: Which Azure Storage services does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake Storage Gen2.

faq.question: Can it help choose between hot, cool, cold, and archive tiers?
faq.answer: Yes. It summarizes access tiers and helps map them to access frequency and retention needs.

faq.question: Does it include SDK examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes quick references for common Azure SDKs across Python, TypeScript, Java, Rust, Go, and .NET contexts.

faq.question: Does it require Azure MCP?
faq.answer: No. It prefers Azure MCP when enabled, but it also includes Azure CLI fallback guidance.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production security decisions?
faq.answer: It helps with implementation guidance, but production designs should still follow tenant policy, RBAC reviews, and Microsoft documentation.
